text: Single-cell data integration can provide a comprehensive molecular view of cells. However, how to integrate heterogeneous single-cell multi-omics as well as spatially resolved transcriptomic data remains a major challenge. Here we introduce uniPort, a unified single-cell data integration framework that combines a coupled variational autoencoder (coupled-VAE) and minibatch unbalanced optimal transport (Minibatch-UOT). It leverages both highly variable common and dataset-specific genes for integration to handle the heterogeneity across datasets, and it is scalable to large-scale datasets. uniPort jointly embeds heterogeneous single-cell multi-omics datasets into a shared latent space. It can further construct a reference atlas for gene imputation across datasets. Meanwhile, uniPort provides a flexible label transfer framework to deconvolute heterogeneous spatial transcriptomic data using an optimal transport plan, instead of embedding latent space. We demonstrate the capability of uniPort by applying it to integrate a variety of datasets, including single-cell transcriptomics, chromatin accessibility, and spatially resolved transcriptomic data.

The latest developments in high-throughput single-cell multi-omics sequencing technologies enable comprehensive studies of heterogeneous cell populations. However, tremendous efforts have been devoted to integrating multiple datasets simultaneously profiled from the same cells (e.g., paired-cell datasets generated by CITE-seq), while it is technically challenging and costly to obtain such paired data universally. Therefore, a vast number of methods have been developed for data profiled from different cells. Among existing single-cell integration methods, Seurat projected feature space into a common subspace using canonical correlation analysis (CCA), LIGER and DC3 employed non-negative matrix factorization, and Harmony provided a latent space via iterative clustering. However, these methods rely on linear operations, lacking the ability to handle nonlinear deformations across cellular modalities. To address these shortcomings, uniPort combines a coupled variational autoencoder (coupled-VAE) and Minibatch Unbalanced Optimal Transport (Minibatch-UOT). This framework allows leveraging both highly variable common and dataset-specific genes for integration in order to handle the heterogeneity across datasets.

uniPort takes diverse and heterogeneous single-cell datasets across different modalities as input. It is based on a coupled variational autoencoder (coupled-VAE) framework. The Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) loss for the coupled-VAE is given by: $\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}}=E_{\psi(z\mid x)}[\log\phi(x\mid z)]-\lambda D_{KL}(\psi(z\mid x)\| p(z))$. The ELBO consists of a reconstruction term and a KL divergence regularization term. We set the prior distribution to be the centered isotropic multivariate Gaussian $p(z)=\mathcal{N}(z; 0, I)$. For two single-cell datasets $X$ and $Y$, we select top $k$ highly variable common genes to form $X^{c}$ and $Y^{c}$, and top $k_x$ and $k_y$ highly variable genes to form dataset-specific sets $X^{s}$ and $Y^{s}$. The modified ELBO' loss is: $\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}}= E_{\psi(z\mid x)}\left\{\log\phi(x\mid z)+\lambda_{s}\left(\log\phi_{x}\left(x^{s}\mid z\right)+\log\phi_{y}\left(y^{s}\mid z\right)\right)\right\}-\lambda D_{\text{KL}}(\psi(z\mid x)\| p(z))$. To better integrate heterogeneous datasets in the latent space, we design an alignment term using Minibatch-UOT.

The Minibatch-UOT cost between cell $x_i$ and $y_j$ is defined as: $C_{i j}=\left\|\mu_{x_i}-\mu_{y_j}\right\|^2+\left\|\sigma_{x_i}-\sigma_{y_j}\right\|^2$. We compute the optimal Minibatch-UOT plan $T^*$ by solving: $T^*=\underset{T\in R_*^{B_X\times B_y}}{\operatorname{argmin}}<C, T>-\epsilon H(T)+\rho D_{K L}\left(T 1_{B_y}\|a\right)+\rho D_{K L}\left(T^{\top} 1_{B_x}\|b\right)$. Here, $<C, T>=\sum_{i, j} C_{i j} T_{i j}$, and the entropy regularization term is $H(T)= -\sum_{i, j} T_{i j}(\log T_{i j}-1)$. Vectors $a$ and $b$ are marginal distributions. The optimal plan is found via an inexact proximal point method (IPOT): $\alpha^{(l+1)}=\left(\frac{a}{G\beta^{(l)}}\right)^{\frac{\rho}{\rho+\varepsilon}},\beta^{(l+1)}=\left(\frac{b}{G^{\top}\alpha^{(l)}}\right)^{\frac{\rho}{\rho+\varepsilon}}$, where $G_{i j}=T_{i j}^{(l)} e^{-C_{i j}/\epsilon}$. The Minibatch-UOT loss is $\mathcal{L}_{UOT^{*}}=\sum_{i, j} C_{i j}\times T_{i j}^{*}$. The total loss function minimized by uniPort is $\mathcal{L}_{\text{uniPort}}=-\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}}+\gamma\mathcal{L}_{\text{UOT}}$.

We benchmarked uniPort against state-of-the-art methods on scATAC and scRNA datasets. uniPort accurately integrated paired and unpaired datasets, achieving high Silhouette coefficients (e.g., 0.64 for paired PBMC) and Batch Entropy scores. For spatially resolved transcriptomics, uniPort was applied to integrate MERFISH and scRNA data. It outperformed other methods in identifying cell types like OD Immature cells. Furthermore, uniPort can impute missing genes in MERFISH from scRNA data. By training a reference atlas, uniPort imputed testing genes with the highest median Spearman correlation coefficients (mSCC of 0.259) and Pearson correlation coefficients (mPCC of 0.249), significantly above gimVI and Tangram. This demonstrates its ability to enhance the resolution of spatial transcriptomics.

uniPort provides a flexible label transfer framework to deconvolute heterogeneous spatial transcriptomic data using the optimal transport plan. We applied uniPort to deconvolute synthetic STARmap data and real-world 10x Visium data of the mouse brain. uniPort accurately reconstructed well-structured cortical layers (L2/3-L6) and separated subpopulations like oligodendrocytes and astrocytes, consistent with anatomical annotations. In a HER2-positive breast cancer dataset, uniPort harmonized diverse modalities and identified Tertiary Lymphoid Structures (TLS) via colocalization of T and B cells. For pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), uniPort revealed cancer heterogeneity by decomposing malignant cells into clone A and B subtypes, correlating with pathways like platelet activation and PI3K signaling. These results demonstrate that uniPort can decipher canonical structures and reveal cancer heterogeneity across tissues.

uniPort tackles several computational challenges by combining coupled-VAE and Minibatch-UOT. It removes the constraint of paired cells required by other autoencoder models. Unlike existing methods that only consider common genes, uniPort leverages dataset-specific genes to capture cell-type heterogeneity. The framework is computationally efficient and scalable to large-scale datasets, addressing the high computational cost of global optimal transport methods. By introducing Minibatch-UOT into a VAE-based framework, uniPort only needs to solve a mini-batch transport plan at each iteration, significantly reducing computational cost. We demonstrate that uniPort consistently performs favorably when compared with recently published state-of-the-art methods and successfully deconvolutes spatial heterogeneous data using the OT plan, offering a unified solution for single-cell multi-omics integration.
